title: Cirrus CI replacement
body:
Cirrus CI will shut down on June 1, https://cirruslabs.org/.

Because we heavily depend on Cirrus CI we will need to move out CI workflows elsewhere. This issue is to track our plan and work to do so.


### Current setup

The [.cirrus.yml](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/.cirrus.yml) file defines all out task. We primarily used our own GCE/AWS infrastructure behind to actually execute the tasks on cloud instances which get automatically created/removed by cirrus CI for each task.
To avoid runtime installs which a) make the tests slower and b) can cause random breakages on all PRs if a dep changes, we instead build "static" cloud images with all deps installed and then use these as testing base. The infra to build these is in https://github.com/containers/automation_images/ which uses packager to create these images.

For the majority of tasks we just use small x86_64 linux VM instances, we do however also test on aarch64 so we need such instances as well.
Then we have podman machine tests which is being tested on bare metal linux (x86_64, aarch64), windows, and macos (mac mini). The bare metal machines are needed as podman machine itself must be able to create and run VMs.

### Requirements 

- be able to define the same tasks as we used to have in cirrus, that means task dependency support where one test wait for another before running. Support for dynamic test skips base don files changed in a PR, i.e. to avoid running all expensive and slow test when docs are changed we have rules which trigger based on which files got changed.

- publish artifacts somewhere, both log files and binary builds that will be stored fore a while (90 days currently)

- run x86_64 and aarch64 linux VMs with static VM images (based on on fedora/debian) that have all deps included to avoid runtime installs

- bare metal macos (aarch64), windows (x86_64) and linux (x86_64 and aarch64) system to perform machine tests, if there are systems with nested virt it may be possible to use them for machine tests. Though the tests are slow so we need to ensure they would have acceptable runtime



### Plan

(not complete, my personal opinion)

The most logic thing would be likely to move to github actions, many more people are familiar with it and task definition wise it should be able to do most things. In order to run fedora/debian images it should be possible to boot them as VM on a github action runner.

It also seems CNCF recommends github actions, or at least most projects use it there AFAICT.
If we use github actions here we likely hit the free limits quickly, CNCF offers an enterprise org so we likely need to move to a new org to take advantage of it for higher limits? They also offer custom github action runners to use https://github.com/cncf/automation/blob/main/ci/README.md#custom-runners

For podman machine testing we need to figure out if we can get bare metal macos and windows runners. It might also be worth to check with Podman Desktop on this as they also need to test/run podman machine.

comments.body: cc @containers/podman-maintainers @timcoding1988 @actionmancan
/assign

(@thrix, PTAL and correct me if i'm wrong on any of this..)

My main suggestion would be to have jobs triggerable via TMT .. 

- TMT jobs can be triggered using Packit, GitLab CI for RHEL. There's also a [GitHub Action](https://github.com/marketplace/actions/schedule-tests-on-testing-farm) to run them on testing-farm (TF), so shouldn't block any switch to GHA. I think this would be a huge reusability benefit.
- TF jobs **can** use static composes (we build an AMI and share it with TF's account, IIUC). There's also a WIP for alternative workflow.
- TF jobs via GitHub actions don't have to depend on packit / copr rpms. They could directly build and test the source as is.
- Not sure about debian composes and also logs and artifact visibility duration. Miroslav, wdyt?

> TMT jobs can be triggered using Packit, GitLab CI for RHEL. There's also a [GitHub Action](https://github.com/marketplace/actions/schedule-tests-on-testing-farm) to run them on testing-farm (TF), so shouldn't block any switch to GHA. I think this would be a huge reusability benefit.

How can they be safely triggered on regular external PRs? If we need to get access to the `api_key` secret as part of the wrokflow it means one cannot use the regular pull_request trigger. And if you do not use the PR trigger you cannot modify/test workflow changes on the PR as it always would only run on the file content of main.


> TF jobs via GitHub actions don't have to depend on packit / copr rpms. They could directly build and test the source as is.

The github action says:

Currently only testing of copr builds is supported by the action. See [Testing Farm docs](https://docs.testing-farm.io/) for more information on supported test artifacts which Testing > > Farm can install into the environment.

---

And AFAIK testing farm has no windows/macos support? So we would still need something else there? And CNCF wise I guess I rather consolidate towards the CNCF "tooling".

---

Overall I think packit/tmt as is can be useful but it is not the best choice for our main CI tasks. Just speaking from current experience of the tasks they are broken way to often for totally unclear reasons. I am not saying that is anyone particular fault but I see things like broken build chroot errors, broken dependencies and many other things on PRs.

Just one example https://github.com/containers/podman/runs/71660449651, I have no idea what is even wrong there as findings the right logs is just so confusing in tmt IMO.

> How can they be safely triggered on regular external PRs? If we need to get access to the `api_key` secret as part of the wrokflow it means one cannot use the regular pull_request trigger.

@thrix how is the api_key access controlled? Once api_key is stored in the repo's secrets, can it be safely reused for all PRs without external contributors requiring access, or does everyone need to have their own key for PRs ? 

-----

> Currently only testing of copr builds is supported by the action. See [Testing Farm docs](https://docs.testing-farm.io/) for more information on supported test artifacts which Testing > > Farm can install into the environment.

@thrix is that still the case? Is there no equivalent of a `skip_build: true` like in packit?

-----

> And AFAIK testing farm has no windows/macos support? So we would still need something else there? And CNCF wise I guess I rather consolidate towards the CNCF "tooling".

True, but if CNCF tooling is GHA, then that shouldn't be a problem with testing-farm ?? The solution I have in mind right now does *not* include Packit. Only TMT and testing-farm. 

-----

> Overall I think packit/tmt as is can be useful but it is not the best choice for our main CI tasks. Just speaking from current experience of the tasks they are broken way to often for totally unclear reasons. I am not saying that is anyone particular fault but I see things like broken build chroot errors, broken dependencies and many other things on PRs.
 
> Just one example https://github.com/containers/podman/runs/71660449651, I have no idea what is even wrong there as findings the right logs is just so confusing in tmt IMO.

That is largely because we haven't used static composes for the testing-farm jobs triggered via `Packit`. If we resort to using GHA + static composes (like we do for Cirrus), that should not happen.

>> Overall I think packit/tmt as is can be useful but it is not the best choice for our main CI tasks. Just speaking from current experience of the tasks they are broken way to often for totally unclear reasons. I am not saying that is anyone particular fault but I see things like broken build chroot errors, broken dependencies and many other things on PRs.
>> 
>> Just one example https://github.com/containers/podman/runs/71660449651, I have no idea what is even wrong there as findings the right logs is just so confusing in tmt IMO.

> That is largely because we haven't used static composes for the testing-farm jobs triggered via Packit. If we resort to using GHA + static composes (like we do for Cirrus), that should not happen.

I checked with my colleagues in the office, and they haven't experienced those problems with packit/tmt/tf. They think that doesn't use any static composes. I got a suggestion that we could use `tf_extra_params` in the config to limit the number of parallel jobs. Maybe that could help.

https://github.com/ComplianceAsCode/content/blob/master/.packit.yaml#L37-L40

> > How can they be safely triggered on regular external PRs? If we need to get access to the `api_key` secret as part of the wrokflow it means one cannot use the regular pull_request trigger.
> 
> [@thrix](https://github.com/thrix) how is the api_key access controlled? Once api_key is stored in the repo's secrets, can it be safely reused for all PRs without external contributors requiring access, or does everyone need to have their own key for PRs ?

My point is that repo secrets are not available in the workflow when trigger by pull_request, you would need pull_request_target which is easy to miss configure and leak the api keys to any external contributor. Where possible it should be avoided for general CI tasks since it always uses the workflow from the target branch so you cannot test changes in PRs. https://stackoverflow.com/questions/74957218/what-is-the-difference-between-pull-request-and-pull-request-target-event-in-git

> True, but if CNCF tooling is GHA, then that shouldn't be a problem with testing-farm ?? The solution I have in mind right now does not include Packit. Only TMT and testing-farm.

Well yes but the infra would not be owned by CNCF. I think there is some interest in actually having the full CI infra owned and paid for by CNCF.

> That is largely because we haven't used static composes for the testing-farm jobs triggered via Packit. If we resort to using GHA + static composes (like we do for Cirrus), that should not happen.

Yes that could be the case, hard to know what the error rate looks like with static composes.

> Overall I think packit/tmt as is can be useful but it is not the best choice for our main CI tasks. Just speaking from current experience of the tasks they are broken way to often for totally unclear reasons. I am not saying that is anyone particular fault but I see things like broken build chroot errors, broken dependencies and many other things on PRs.
> 
> Just one example https://github.com/containers/podman/runs/71660449651, I have no idea what is even wrong there as findings the right logs is just so confusing in tmt IMO.

Yeah, we should display tmt log in this case (the error mentions check the logs, and we should document better which logs to check in [our docs](https://docs.testing-farm.io/Testing%20Farm/0.1/test-results.html#additional-logs), this one seems to be missing):

```
Cause number 1:

    prepare step failed

    The exception was caused by 1 earlier exceptions

    Cause number 1:

        Command 'set -eo pipefail; bash /var/ARTIFACTS/work-remote-rootlessd3ecfel8/plans/system/remote-rootless/tree/contrib/packit-tmt/dnf-repo-setup.sh' returned 2.

        # stderr (2 lines)
        # ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
        + dnf -y upgrade --allowerasing --disable-repo=testing-farm-tag-repository '--exclude=podman*'
        No matching repositories for testing-farm-tag-repository. Add "--help" for more information about the arguments.
        # ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

The podman's prepare step is relying on `testing-farm-tag-repository` repository to exist. Unfortunately it can happen it is not available.  We are trying to improve this environment setup step to be more stable here:

https://gitlab.com/testing-farm/infrastructure/-/merge_requests/1328

But ideally it should be treated as possibly not available. That should make this one go away.

Do you have any other example of unreadable error?

> Do you have any other example of unreadable error?

Not off hand, it is certainly possible that most I saw there are the same error as you shown here.
I guess when I look at packit there is the other part of rpm build failures in copr though that is not tmt related then.

All I am trying to say is the packit/copr/tmt/etc... stack we have on existing PRs is failing a lot more than I would like. I do believe these are good tools and tmt is especially interesting with the easy local runs. But I am not convinced it is the right tool for our main blocking upstream CI which also needs support for debian, windows and macos. Is tmt disto agnostic?

> > How can they be safely triggered on regular external PRs? If we need to get access to the `api_key` secret as part of the wrokflow it means one cannot use the regular pull_request trigger.
> 
> [@thrix](https://github.com/thrix) how is the api_key access controlled? Once api_key is stored in the repo's secrets, can it be safely reused for all PRs without external contributors requiring access, or does everyone need to have their own key for PRs ?

I am recently learning that with GitHub this is quite a pain point in regards to contributions from forks. In GitLab one has merge request pipelines which the maintainers approve from non-project contributors and they run with the repository secrets on the PR code. In GitHub it is not so straight forward.

The current advised way of dealing with this is to:

1. for external contributors - have a separate privileged workflow and trigger it when a project owner/member/collaborator adds a comment (basically approves the change).

2. for project contributors use `pull_request` to trigger the tests automatically

See this example I am preparing:

https://github.com/thrix/nix-toolbox/pull/45

> 
> > Currently only testing of copr builds is supported by the action. See [Testing Farm docs](https://docs.testing-farm.io/) for more information on supported test artifacts which Testing > > Farm can install into the environment.
> 
> [@thrix](https://github.com/thrix) is that still the case? Is there no equivalent of a `skip_build: true` like in packit?
> 
> > And AFAIK testing farm has no windows/macos support? So we would still need something else there? And CNCF wise I guess I rather consolidate towards the CNCF "tooling".

Not yet, but it is on the roadmap. If this would be the only major thing we can try to prioritize it.

> 
> True, but if CNCF tooling is GHA, then that shouldn't be a problem with testing-farm ?? The solution I have in mind right now does _not_ include Packit. Only TMT and testing-farm.

Yep, I would base the solution on GHA, and use tmt and testing-farm where obviously a good candidate.

> 
> > Overall I think packit/tmt as is can be useful but it is not the best choice for our main CI tasks. Just speaking from current experience of the tasks they are broken way to often for totally unclear reasons. I am not saying that is anyone particular fault but I see things like broken build chroot errors, broken dependencies and many other things on PRs.
> 
> > Just one example https://github.com/containers/podman/runs/71660449651, I have no idea what is even wrong there as findings the right logs is just so confusing in tmt IMO.
> 
> That is largely because we haven't used static composes for the testing-farm jobs triggered via `Packit`. If we resort to using GHA + static composes (like we do for Cirrus), that should not happen.

Indeed, with static composes and our new functionality to [completely skip the test environment preparation](https://docs.testing-farm.io/Testing%20Farm/0.1/test-request.html#skip-guest-setup) it should not be as stable as possible.

> > Do you have any other example of unreadable error?
> 
> Not off hand, it is certainly possible that most I saw there are the same error as you shown here. I guess when I look at packit there is the other part of rpm build failures in copr though that is not tmt related then.
> 
> All I am trying to say is the packit/copr/tmt/etc... stack we have on existing PRs is failing a lot more than I would like. I do believe these are good tools and tmt is especially interesting with the easy local runs. But I am not convinced it is the right tool for our main blocking upstream CI which also needs support for debian, windows and macos. Is tmt disto agnostic?

I am not trying to change your mind, just trying to improve our tools. It is prepared to be distro agnostic:

https://github.com/teemtee/tmt/tree/main/tmt/package_managers

But we are missing exactly projects interested in this to bring it into a production grade state.

But the Windows and MacOS support is something we need to work on :(

> I think there is some interest in actually having the full CI infra owned and paid for by CNCF.

@Luap99 is monetary cost a reason for this? FWIW, testing-farm usage will be no extra monetary cost to this team, or to CNCF.

> > I think there is some interest in actually having the full CI infra owned and paid for by CNCF.
> 
> [@Luap99](https://github.com/Luap99) is monetary cost a reason for this? FWIW, testing-farm usage will be no extra monetary cost to this team, or to CNCF.

Yeah, we are sponsored by Amazon itself.

As a CNCF project, we need to be using CNCF infrastructure if we wish to move towards milestones like incubation. I don't think using the CNCF-provided infra can be up for debate given that.

That said, it does not provide a solution for some of our hardest problems - bare-metal Windows and Mac, certainly.

> As a CNCF project, we need to be using CNCF infrastructure if we wish to move towards milestones like incubation. I don't think using the CNCF-provided infra can be up for debate given that.
> 
> That said, it does not provide a solution for some of our hardest problems - bare-metal Windows and Mac, certainly.

Not to say we tried to get a Mac instances :( https://forge.fedoraproject.org/infra/tickets/issues/12058. Slowly 2 years.

Anyway, sooner or later Windows is coming, we have other users asking for that.

But yeah, sorry to say we cannot provide that right now.

> be able to define the same tasks as we used to have in cirrus, that means task dependency support where one test wait for another before running. Support for dynamic test skips base don files changed in a PR, i.e. to avoid running all expensive and slow test when docs are changed we have rules which trigger based on which files got changed.

https://github.com/dorny/paths-filter looks popular, but I haven't dug in much.

> bare metal macos (aarch64), windows (x86_64) and linux (x86_64 and aarch64) system to perform machine tests, if there are systems with nested virt it may be possible to use them for machine tests. Though the tests are slow so we need to ensure they would have acceptable runtime

GHA default runners (Azure) on x86_64 supports nested virt, we use it extensively for bootc. Not on aarch64 though, which is a bit annoying.

But, a decent pattern is:

- Sanity testing using nested virt on x86_64 on each PR
- Postsubmit or opt-in additional testing on aarch64 running on metal instances

That said, Azure seems to [support ARM nested virt](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/2245449/how-to-create-an-arm-vm-with-nested-virtualization) but apparently that hasn't propagated to GHA yet.

---

That said I think the reliability boot of using CNCF infra is conditional on the GHA org move right? Which I guess was stuck on CI, but if cirrus is going away that forces the issue?

---

As far as Testing Farm, it makes sense to use, but I wouldn't use it on every PR for all tests necessarily. It does force the TMT thing which is good, but I also think we need to have tooling that maps e.g. to https://wiki.debian.org/ContinuousIntegration/autopkgtest and others too.

> GHA default runners (Azure) on x86_64 supports nested virt

Can confirm this. I've been testing forked version of Podman using Windows runners this way for a while.

> That said I think the reliability boot of using CNCF infra is conditional on the GHA org move right? Which I guess was stuck on CI, but if cirrus is going away that forces the issue?



> > be able to define the same tasks as we used to have in cirrus, that means task dependency support where one test wait for another before running. Support for dynamic test skips base don files changed in a PR, i.e. to avoid running all expensive and slow test when docs are changed we have rules which trigger based on which files got changed.
> 
> https://github.com/dorny/paths-filter looks popular, but I haven't dug in much.
> 

yeah looks like the GH action only offers the path filter at the global pr trigger https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/workflow-syntax#onpushpull_requestpull_request_targetpathspaths-ignore

Not sure why they don't allow that per job but yeah this action seem to solve that.

> > bare metal macos (aarch64), windows (x86_64) and linux (x86_64 and aarch64) system to perform machine tests, if there are systems with nested virt it may be possible to use them for machine tests. Though the tests are slow so we need to ensure they would have acceptable runtime
> 
> GHA default runners (Azure) on x86_64 supports nested virt, we use it extensively for bootc. Not on aarch64 though, which is a bit annoying.
> 
> But, a decent pattern is:
> 
>     * Sanity testing using nested virt on x86_64 on each PR
> 
>     * Postsubmit or opt-in additional testing on aarch64 running on metal instances
> 
> 
> That said, Azure seems to [support ARM nested virt](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/2245449/how-to-create-an-arm-vm-with-nested-virtualization) but apparently that hasn't propagated to GHA yet.

Did you try to use the CNCF runners on oracle cloud https://github.com/cncf/automation/blob/main/ci/README.md#custom-runners? Maybe they can already do nested virt on arm?

per https://blogs.oracle.com/linux/kvm-nested-virtualization-in-oci I guess not but maybe something changed
> Ampere VMs (ARM/aarch64) did not support nested virtualization


> That said I think the reliability boot of using CNCF infra is conditional on the GHA org move right? Which I guess was stuck on CI, but if cirrus is going away that forces the issue?


Pretty much, yes.


> 
> As far as Testing Farm, it makes sense to use, but I wouldn't use it on every PR for all tests necessarily. It does force the TMT thing which is good, but I also think we need to have tooling that maps e.g. to https://wiki.debian.org/ContinuousIntegration/autopkgtest and others too.

Sorry I don't quite follow what you are trying to say with that? I am not sure how the debian testing infra comes in play here.

I think overall out test suites should be able to get run with a simple make target (which they are) and then the CI testing definitions itself just have to call that target and ensure deps are available and setup things like a regular user or root depending on how the test suite should be run (normally one job as root and one as rootless).

---

> > GHA default runners (Azure) on x86_64 supports nested virt
> 
> Can confirm this. I've been testing forked version of Podman using Windows runners this way for a while.

So with the default windows GH action runner we can run podman machine just fine? This would certainly be great as it only leaves us with the macos problem then. And well macos even right now is special as it is just a pool of self hosted mac mini runners with cirrus so doing the same for github actions should not be to big of a problem.

> So with the default windows GH action runner we can run podman machine just fine?

I have access only to basic sized runners in my private repo, so, it is not that fast. I also have to rely on default images and install everything every time. With the organization there should be more options to optimize the runners for workloads.

Here are logs from my built of Podman 6 dev for Windows x86_64 with e2e machine tests from this week https://github.com/arixmkii/qcw/actions/runs/24495225798/job/71588539612

Minikube is a CNCF project. Minikube creates VMs to run kube on Linux, macOS and Windows. So pretty similar to Podman machines use case (minikube runs on Hyper-V and Hyperkit).

For example on Windows they run:
- [unit tests with the GitHub windows-2022](https://github.com/kubernetes/minikube/blob/master/.github/workflows/unit-test.yml#L48)
- [e2e tests](https://github.com/kubernetes/minikube/tree/master/test/integration) on an [azure VM](https://github.com/kubernetes/minikube/blob/master/hack/windows-ci-image/vm.bicep)  running Windows 11 every 12 hours on main branch and on labelled PR ([the gh workflow](https://github.com/kubernetes/minikube/blob/799c248d9faccedf7fc6af377bb4464dbebf431f/.github/workflows/functional_extra.yml#L16)).

GitHub actions are what most of the CNCF projects use and would help contributions IMHO.

Lima is another CNCF project. They run e2e tests on Windows using GHA.

* WLS2 https://github.com/lima-vm/lima/blob/ef60e28ed6feb75618ae645bb4e38128d1a21f81/.github/workflows/test.yml#L155
* QEMU (WHPX accelerated, so, using nested virt) https://github.com/lima-vm/lima/blob/ef60e28ed6feb75618ae645bb4e38128d1a21f81/.github/workflows/test.yml#L208
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@Luap99  should we close this issue ?
yes, follow up work is tracked here https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/issues/28824
